elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we extend recent work of the third author and Ziegler on triples of integers $(a,b,c)$, with the property that each of $(a,b,c)$, $(a+1,b+1,c+1)$ and $(a+2,b+2,c+2)$ is {\it multiplicatively dependent , completely classifying such triples in case $a=2$.}
elements.sent-2411-002: Our techniques include a variety of elementary arguments together with more involved machinery from Diophantine approximation.
elements.sent-2411-003: An $n$-tuple of numbers $(z_1, \ldots, z_n) \in \C^n$ is said to be multiplicatively dependent, if there exists a non-zero tuple $(k_1, \ldots, k_n) \in \Z^n$ such that \begin{equation}z_1^{k_1} \cdots z_n^{k_n} = 1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we will consider tuples $(z_1, \ldots, z_n)$ of positive integers and, for simplicity, assume $1 < z_1 < \dots < z_n$.
elements.sent-2411-005: Of course, there exist many multiplicatively dependent tuples $(z_1, \ldots, z_n)$; see \cite{PappalardiShaShparlinskiStewart2018 for asymptotics.}
elements.sent-2411-006: We will focus our attention on ``consecutive'' tuples, by which we mean pairs of tuples of the shape $(z_1, \ldots, z_n)$ and $(z_1 + 1, \ldots, z_n + 1)$.
elements.sent-2411-007: For pairs of integers, the situation is rather simple: If $1<a<b$, then $(a,b)$ is multiplicative dependent if and only if it is of the shape $(a,b) = (q^x, q^y)$ for some integers $q>1$ and $1 \leq x<y$.
